cards.answer: A deflagration is ordinary flame: it burns subsonically, heat conducts ahead to ignite the next layer, and pressure stays roughly constant. A detonation is a supersonic combustion front driven by a shock wave that compresses and ignites the mixture, raising pressure sharply across the front. Detonation releases the same chemical energy far faster and with a pressure gain.
cards.hook: Deflagration shuffles like a slow line passing a note forward, word to word, but detonation is a shove: a shock wave slams the door open and the fire has to keep up.
cards.reverse: Name the two combustion modes distinguished by whether the front moves subsonic with roughly constant pressure or supersonic with a sharp pressure spike driven by a shock.

cards.answer: A shock wave tightly coupled to a reaction zone: the shock heats the gas enough to ignite it, and the heat release drives the shock forward. The two travel together as a self sustaining supersonic front, moving at thousands of meters per second in typical fuel and oxidizer mixtures.
cards.hook: Picture two dancers locked arm in arm sprinting together, the shock is the lead pulling the reaction zone along, neither can outrun the other or the pair falls apart.
cards.reverse: What term describes a shock and its trailing combustion zone moving together as one self sustaining supersonic front at thousands of meters per second.

cards.answer: The reference state of an ideal, self sustaining detonation, where the burned gas leaves the reaction zone moving exactly at its local speed of sound relative to the wave. It fixes the minimum stable detonation speed and gives the peak CJ pressure and temperature that the rest of the cycle decays from.
cards.hook: CJ, think Cruising Jet, the exhaust gas leaves exactly at Mach 1 relative to the wave, the slowest speed the wave can still sustain itself at.
cards.reverse: What is the reference state where burned combustion products exit a detonation front moving at exactly the local sonic speed relative to the wave, fixing minimum wave speed plus peak pressure and temperature.

cards.answer: The Zeldovich, von Neumann, Doring model of what is inside a detonation: an almost infinitely thin shock jump that spikes pressure, followed by a finite length reaction zone where the compressed gas actually burns down to the CJ state. It is the standard one dimensional picture used to compute detonation properties.
cards.hook: ZND, think of it as a spike then a slope: a razor thin pressure spike (shock) followed by a ramp where the gas actually finishes burning down to the CJ state.
cards.reverse: Name the one dimensional model of a detonation's internal structure that pairs an infinitesimally thin shock jump with a finite reaction zone trailing behind it.

cards.answer: The width of the diamond shaped pattern a detonation wave traces on a soot foil, a fingerprint of how easily a given mixture detonates. Smaller lambda means a more detonable mixture. An RDE channel gap and diameter are sized as multiples of lambda so the wave has room to stay stable.
cards.hook: Soot foil diamonds, like fish scales on a windshield, smaller scales mean the mixture is jumpier and easier to detonate, and engine geometry gets sized in multiples of that scale.
cards.reverse: What quantity, measured from the diamond pattern left on a soot foil, indicates how readily a mixture detonates and sets the minimum channel gap and diameter of a rotating detonation engine.

cards.answer: The runaway process by which an ordinary flame accelerates, generates shocks ahead of itself, and abruptly flips into a detonation. Engineers either exploit it to start a detonation or design against it where an unplanned detonation would be dangerous.
cards.hook: DDT is a flame throwing a tantrum, it accelerates, punches shocks out ahead of itself, and snaps into a full detonation, sometimes on purpose, sometimes as the thing you design against.
cards.reverse: Name the runaway process in which an accelerating ordinary flame generates shocks ahead of it and abruptly converts into a detonation wave.

cards.answer: The actual fuel to oxidizer ratio divided by the exact stoichiometric ratio. Phi equal to 1 is stoichiometric, Phi above 1 is fuel rich, Phi below 1 is fuel lean. It is the single knob that sets flame temperature, detonability, and how much unburned propellant is left over.
cards.hook: Phi like a dial: crank it past 1 and you are drowning the fire in fuel (rich), dial it under 1 and the fire is starving (lean), right at 1 it is fed exactly right.
cards.reverse: What single ratio, actual fuel to oxidizer divided by the stoichiometric value, sets flame temperature, detonability, and leftover unburned propellant.

cards.answer: Thrust produced per unit weight flow of propellant per second, measured in seconds. It is the fuel economy of a rocket engine: a higher Isp means more thrust for the same propellant burned. Detonation engines chase higher Isp through their pressure gain.
cards.hook: Isp is a rocket's miles per gallon, except the gallon is a pound of propellant burned each second and the answer comes out in seconds, not miles.

cards.answer: A performance number that measures how well the combustion chamber alone turns propellant into hot, high pressure gas, independent of the nozzle. It depends only on the propellant chemistry and chamber conditions, and it feeds directly into mass flow and thrust calculations.
cards.hook: C-star, the chamber's report card before the nozzle even gets involved, it only grades how well the combustion turned propellant into hot pressurized gas.
cards.reverse: What performance figure, dependent only on propellant chemistry and chamber conditions, measures how effectively a combustion chamber alone converts propellant into hot high pressure gas, independent of the nozzle.

cards.answer: Any combustion mode that leaves the gas at a higher stagnation pressure than it entered with, instead of losing pressure the way constant pressure burning does. It is the core thermodynamic reason to build a rotating detonation engine at all.
cards.hook: Most combustion is a toll booth, you lose pressure passing through, pressure gain combustion is a tailwind, you come out the other side with more push than you entered with.
cards.reverse: What is the general name for any combustion mode that raises the gas's stagnation pressure rather than lowering it, the core thermodynamic motive for building a rotating detonation engine.

cards.answer: The RDE combustion channel is a ring shaped gap, the annulus, between an outer wall and an inner cylinder called the center body. The detonation wave races around this ring. The center body typically absorbs more heat than the outer wall because the flow impinges on it more directly.
cards.hook: Picture a donut, the ring of dough is the annulus where the detonation wave races laps, the hole in the middle is the center body, and the inner edge takes the harsher beating from direct flow impingement.
cards.reverse: What two structures form the ring shaped combustion channel of a rotating detonation engine, an outer wall paired with an inner cylinder, and which typically absorbs more heat.

cards.answer: After the detonation wave passes a point on the wall, fresh propellant refills the channel behind it before the wave comes around again. The axial height this fresh charge reaches is the fill height, and the cooler refill zone gives the wall a brief regenerative style cooling window each cycle.
cards.hook: Right after the wave passes, fresh cool air rushes in behind it like a draft through a closing door, that draft's reach up the wall is the fill height, and it briefly cools the wall each lap.
cards.reverse: What terms describe the axial extent fresh reactant reaches after a detonation wave passes a wall location, and the resulting periodic cooling window before the wave returns.

cards.answer: How fast the detonation travels around the annulus, and how many waves are present at once. Multiple co-rotating waves raise the apparent frequency and can make a naive single-wave speed estimate come out too high, so engineers measure both directly with spaced pressure transducers.
cards.hook: One racer looks fast at a single gate, but if three racers are on the track together the gate double counts them, that is why you need multiple sensors, not one clock.
cards.reverse: What two quantities, the travel rate of a detonation around the annulus and how many fronts are present at once, must be measured with spaced transducers rather than inferred from a single sensor.

cards.answer: The temperature a wall would reach if it exchanged no net heat with the flow, set by the gas stagnation temperature minus a small recovery correction for the boundary layer. It is the true driving temperature for wall heat flux: flux is proportional to this recovery temperature minus the actual wall temperature, not to the raw flame temperature.
cards.hook: Recovery temperature is the wall's honest report card, not the flame's bragging rights, it is what the surface would settle to with zero net heat exchange, and it is what actually drives the flux math.
cards.reverse: What temperature, defined as the value a wall reaches with no net heat exchange and used as the true driving temperature in convective heat flux calculations, is the gas stagnation temperature minus a boundary layer correction.

cards.answer: The proportionality constant in q double prime equals h times the gas to wall temperature difference. It lumps all the messy convection physics into one number in watts per square meter kelvin. It is not a material constant: it depends on flow conditions and on which reference gas temperature you pair it with.
cards.hook: h is a translator, not a fixed law, like a currency exchange rate that shifts with the market, it converts a temperature gap into a heat flux but the rate depends on flow conditions and which temperature you quoted it against.
cards.reverse: What proportionality constant, in watts per square meter kelvin, links wall heat flux to the gas to wall temperature difference, and why is it not a fixed material property.

cards.answer: The rate of heat crossing a unit area of surface, in watts per square meter. In an RDE it is fiercely non-uniform: peak instantaneous flux near the channel base can exceed several megawatts per square meter, while the cycle average and the axial tail are far lower.
cards.hook: Picture a firehose blasting one patch of wall near the base while a garden hose trickles at the exit, same channel, wildly different intensities depending on where you stand.
cards.reverse: What quantity, the rate of heat crossing a unit wall area in watts per square meter, spikes to several megawatts near an RDE's channel base while staying far lower on cycle average and toward the exit.

cards.answer: The dimensionless groups of convective heat transfer. Reynolds sets whether flow is laminar or turbulent, Prandtl compares momentum to thermal diffusion in the fluid, Nusselt is dimensionless heat transfer coefficient, and Stanton normalizes convective flux against the flow's own heat carrying capacity. Correlations tie them together to predict h.
cards.hook: Four letters like a family: Reynolds is the rowdy kid deciding laminar versus turbulent, Prandtl compares two siblings (momentum versus heat diffusion), Nusselt is h dressed up dimensionless, and Stanton normalizes flux against the flow's own heat capacity.
cards.reverse: Which four dimensionless numbers of convective heat transfer combine in correlations to predict the heat transfer coefficient, covering flow regime, fluid property ratio, dimensionless h, and flux normalized against flow enthalpy capacity.

cards.answer: A material property, conductivity divided by density times specific heat, that measures how fast a temperature disturbance spreads through a solid rather than how much heat it stores. It governs how quickly a surface heating event shows up at a thermocouple buried a few millimeters deep.
cards.hook: Alpha is not how much heat a wall can hold, it is how fast a temperature blip races through it, like the difference between a bucket's capacity and how quickly a wave crosses the bucket.
cards.reverse: What material property, conductivity divided by density times specific heat, governs how quickly a surface heating event is felt by a thermocouple buried a few millimeters deep, rather than how much total heat the solid stores.

cards.answer: Treating a wall as if it extends infinitely deep, valid for short times before the heat pulse reaches the far side. It gives a clean closed form linking surface temperature history to surface heat flux, and it underlies fast-response heat flux gauge analysis in RDE testing.
cards.hook: Treat the wall like the ocean, a wave lapping the shore never notices the far side of the Pacific, valid as long as you check the clock before the disturbance could reach the back face.
cards.reverse: What simplification treats a solid wall as infinitely deep, valid for short times, and gives a closed form link between surface temperature history and surface heat flux used in fast response gauge analysis.

cards.answer: Three ways to keep an engine wall alive. Regenerative routes propellant through wall passages to carry heat away before injection. Film cooling blankets the wall in a cooler gas layer. Heat sink cooling adds no cooling at all and just lets a thick wall soak up heat for a short test. HADES is heat sink.
cards.hook: Three ways to keep a wall alive: regenerative is drinking your own coolant on the way in, film is wrapping yourself in a wet blanket, heat sink is wearing a thick coat and hoping the test ends before you overheat, exactly HADES's approach.
cards.reverse: Name the three engine wall cooling strategies, one routing propellant through wall passages before injection, one blanketing the wall in cooler gas, and one relying purely on a thick wall absorbing heat for a short duration with no active cooling.

cards.answer: The chemical energy released per unit mass of fuel when it burns and the product water stays vapor, in megajoules per kilogram. Wall heat loads are often reported as a percentage of the propellant LHV, which lets you compare rigs of different size and mass flow on equal footing.
cards.hook: LHV is the fuel's paycheck before taxes, the energy released assuming the water byproduct stays vapor and never gives up its condensation bonus, and wall losses get reported as a percentage cut of that paycheck.
cards.reverse: What quantity, the chemical energy released per unit fuel mass assuming water product stays vapor, is commonly used as the denominator when reporting wall heat loads as a percentage.

cards.answer: Working backward from a temperature measured inside a wall to infer the surface heat flux and temperature you could not measure directly. It is mathematically unstable because reversing diffusion amplifies noise, so real methods need care to stay usable. It is the backbone of the HADES Phase 1 back-calculation of h.
cards.hook: IHCP is detective work run backward, you have the footprint (a buried thermocouple reading) and must reconstruct the crime scene (the hot surface flux), and like any cold case the trail gets noisier the further back you push.
cards.reverse: What backward calculation, mathematically unstable because it amplifies measurement noise, infers surface heat flux and temperature from a temperature history measured inside a wall, forming the backbone of HADES's back calculated heat transfer coefficient.

cards.prompt: Walk me through a project you are proud of.
cards.answer: Lead with HADES: an H2/air rotating detonation engine where you own the thermal side. State the goal in one line (validate the thermal model by back-calculating gas-side h from thermocouple data), your specific contribution, and the result. Keep it to design, test, iterate: what you predicted, what the data said, and what you changed. Numbers over adjectives.
cards.hook: Picture handing them a business card that says HADES: one line goal, your part, the result, no adjectives.

cards.prompt: What is the difference between deflagration and detonation, and why does it matter for an engine?
cards.answer: Deflagration is subsonic flame at roughly constant pressure; detonation is a supersonic shock-coupled front that raises pressure across it. It matters because detonation gives a pressure gain, which raises thermodynamic efficiency and specific impulse over a conventional constant-pressure combustor. The cost is severe unsteady heat loads and hardware stress.
cards.hook: Deflagration crawls like a candle flame; detonation is a shockwave punch that also raises pressure, the punch is the whole point.

cards.prompt: How would you measure heat transfer to a wall you cannot instrument on the hot side?
cards.answer: Bury thermocouples at known depths in the wall, record the temperature rise during a hotfire, then solve the inverse heat conduction problem to march back to the surface flux and temperature. From surface flux and the gas-to-wall temperature difference you back out an effective h. Flag that the inverse step amplifies noise, so sensor depth accuracy and placement close to the hot face matter most.
cards.hook: Buried thermocouples are detectives reading footprints, the buried temperature, to reconstruct a crime, the surface flux, nobody saw happen.

cards.prompt: A test result disagrees with your model. What do you do?
cards.answer: Trust the data first, then find why. Check instrumentation and reduction before blaming physics: sensor calibration, depth, sign conventions, boundary conditions. Then list model assumptions that could close the gap, rank by likely impact, and change one thing at a time. Example: gas radiation inflated your fitted h by about 31 percent until it was accounted for. The loop is the point, not being right the first time.
cards.hook: Data is the eyewitness, your model is the suspect: interrogate the sensor before the physics, radiation once cost you 31 percent.

cards.prompt: Why does a rocket engine wall get so hot, and what sets the heat flux?
cards.answer: Hot combustion gas drives convective flux proportional to h times the recovery temperature minus the wall temperature, plus radiation. The recovery temperature, not the raw flame temperature, is the true driver. Flux is highly non-uniform: in an RDE the channel base can see megawatts per square meter while the axial tail is far cooler.
cards.hook: Flux chases the recovery temperature, not the raw flame temperature, like a dog chasing the scent trail rather than the fire itself.

cards.prompt: Tell me about a time you found a mistake in your own work.
cards.answer: Pick a concrete case: a hard-coded value, a wrong boundary condition, or a unit slip you caught by sanity-checking magnitudes. Show the habit, not the heroics: you cross-checked against an independent estimate (a second code, a hand calc, or literature) and the disagreement flagged the bug. SpaceX wants engineers who audit themselves.
cards.hook: A second independent code is your alibi witness: when two methods disagree, one of them is lying, find which.

cards.prompt: How do you decide a mesh or a result is trustworthy?
cards.answer: Convergence and cross-checks. Refine the mesh until the quantity of interest stops moving within tolerance, then confirm the answer is physical: right order of magnitude, right trends, and agreement with an independent method or published data. A pretty contour plot that nobody sanity-checked is not a result.
cards.hook: Refine the mesh until the number stops wiggling, then make it pass a smell test against an independent answer, a pretty plot alone proves nothing.

cards.prompt: What is factor of safety, and how is safety on life different from safety on stress?
cards.answer: Factor of safety is the margin above the minimum a design must survive. On stress it asks how much higher the load could go before failure at a fixed life. On life it divides the predicted cycles to failure by a knockdown to get a shorter design life. The same number under the two conventions does not mean they agree, because the stress to life curve is steep.
cards.hook: Stress margin is a flat ceiling above you, life margin is a steep cliff below you, the same number means very different distances to falling.

cards.prompt: Why detonation engines if they are so hard to cool?
cards.answer: Because the pressure gain buys efficiency and specific impulse a constant-pressure engine cannot reach, and the hardware is mechanically simpler, no turbopump-fed high-pressure chamber needed to get the same effective cycle. The bet is that the thermal and stability problems are solvable engineering, and characterizing wall heat loads, exactly your work, is how you retire that risk.
cards.hook: Trade the turbopump for the pressure gain, then bet your thermal work is what retires the risk you just accepted.

cards.prompt: Describe your analysis workflow for a thermal-structural problem.
cards.answer: Chain it: a transient thermal analysis gives the time-varying temperature field, that field feeds a static structural analysis for stress and strain, and the cyclic strain feeds a strain-life fatigue estimate. Watch the traps: fatigue tools often ignore temperature-dependent properties, so you correct room-temperature life down for hot strength loss rather than trusting the default.
cards.hook: Thermal feeds structural feeds fatigue, a relay race, and the baton drops if you forget hot metal is weaker metal.

cards.prompt: What would you do in your first month if you did not know the tool we use?
cards.answer: Learn it against a problem I already understand. Reproduce a known result, mine or published, in the new tool so I can trust my setup, then move to the real task. I did this cross-checking a thermal fit in a second code and in OpenFOAM: same problem, independent tool, and the agreement built confidence before I relied on it.
cards.hook: Reproduce a known result first, like tuning a new instrument to a note you already know before you play anything new.

cards.answer: Be specific and honest: the pace of design, test, iterate on real hardware, and that the thermal and combustion problems you already work on map onto their engines. Tie it to the goal chain you actually have: learn aerospace, design and simulate and build, contribute to flight hardware. Avoid platitudes; name the work.
cards.hook: Say design, test, iterate on real hardware, then point at HADES as proof you already live that loop.

cards.prompt: How do you handle being wrong in a design review?
cards.answer: Separate ego from the design. If someone finds a hole, the design got better and that is the goal. State what you missed, what you will change, and by when. Bring your own risk list to the review so the hard questions are ones you already flagged, not ones that ambush you.
cards.hook: A hole found in your design in review is a bullet dodged, bring your own risk list so the ambush never lands.

cards.prompt: What is the hardest tradeoff you have made on a project?
cards.answer: Pick one with real tension: fidelity versus turnaround, for example choosing a reduced-order model that runs in minutes over a full simulation that runs in hundreds of hours, accepting a known percent error because it let you sweep many cases. Explain how you bounded the error so the fast answer was still trustworthy.
cards.hook: Minutes versus hundreds of hours: the reduced-order model is a shortcut you can defend because you measured its error bar first.

cards.prompt: How do you prioritize when everything feels urgent?
cards.answer: By what retires the most risk or unblocks the most people. I keep the goal explicit, define what done means for each task, and do the one that most changes the decision downstream first. Short hard-focused blocks beat scattered multitasking, and I write down what I park so nothing silently drops.
cards.hook: Ask which task moves the needle on the biggest downstream decision, that one jumps the queue.

cards.prompt: Explain a technical concept to someone outside your field.
cards.answer: Demonstrate it live: pick one from your work, like why an engine wall survives a flame hotter than its melting point (short exposure plus a thick heat-sink wall soaking the pulse), and explain it with an everyday analogy and no jargon. The skill they are testing is whether you can align a team, not whether you know the term.
cards.hook: A steel wall hotter than its own melting point survives because the flame visits and leaves before the heat gets deep, like a hand swept through a candle flame.

cards.prompt: What did you learn from a project that failed or stalled?
cards.answer: Show growth, not blame. Name what stalled, the root cause, and the process change you made so it does not repeat: a check you now run, an assumption you now verify, a review you now ask for earlier. Failures that produce a durable habit are worth more than clean successes.
cards.hook: Name the root cause, then name the new checkbox you added so the ghost never comes back.

cards.prompt: How would you reduce the wall heat load in an RDE without active cooling?
cards.answer: Lever the geometry and operating point: run leaner to drop flame temperature, use the fresh-charge refill zone for regenerative-style cooling, thicken or pick a higher-diffusivity wall to spread the pulse, and shorten test duration for a heat-sink design. Quantify each: even a modest drop in recovery temperature moves flux directly since flux scales with the gas-to-wall difference.
cards.hook: Run leaner, use the fill zone's cool breath, fatten the wall, shorten the burn, four levers on the same flux equation.

cards.prompt: What questions do you have for us?
cards.answer: Ask about the work and the loop: how fast does a design change reach a test stand, what does an intern own end to end, and how are analysis predictions checked against test data here. Good questions show you think in terms of design, test, iterate, which is how they build.
cards.hook: Ask how fast a whiteboard sketch becomes a test stand hotfire, that question proves you think in the loop they live by.

cards.prompt: Tell me about working on a team where you disagreed technically.
cards.answer: Pick a real disagreement, describe how you resolved it with data rather than volume: you each stated the assumption behind your position, found the one that could be tested cheaply, and let the result decide. End with what you did once the data pointed away from your view.
cards.hook: Two hypotheses, one cheap test, let the data be the tiebreaker instead of the loudest voice.

cards.answer: Measuring the heat a cooled wall absorbs by the temperature rise of the coolant flowing through it: heat rate equals coolant mass flow times specific heat times outlet minus inlet temperature. It gives a reliable wall-integrated average heat rate, which Theuerkauf reports as a percentage of propellant LHV.
cards.hook: Calor is Latin for heat, so think of a calorimeter as a thermometer for a whole coolant loop: it counts the heat by how much the water warmed up passing through, not by touching the flame directly.
cards.reverse: The technique of finding a cooled wall's absorbed heat rate by multiplying the coolant's flow rate, its specific heat, and its outlet minus inlet temperature rise.

cards.answer: Reporting wall heat absorption as a fraction of the propellant lower heating value, so rigs of different size compare fairly. Theuerkauf found the outer wall took about 2.5 percent and the inner wall about 7.1 percent of LHV, and later work put total wall loss near 20 percent for steady detonation.
cards.hook: Picture the fuel's chemical energy as a pie, the whole LHV budget, and the wall heat loss as the slice taken out before you even get thrust. Theuerkauf found the inner wall ate a bigger slice than the outer.
cards.reverse: Expressing how much energy a wall pulls out of the flow as a share of the fuel's total lower heating value, so rigs of different sizes can be compared on equal footing.

cards.answer: The center body absorbed roughly three times the heat of the outer wall in Theuerkauf's rig, because detonation products impinge more directly on the convergent inner surface near the base. It is a warning that outer-wall instrumentation alone does not tell you the inner body thermal load.
cards.hook: Think of the center body as a bullseye the detonation products slam into head on, while the outer wall just gets grazed, roughly a 3 to 1 heat split in Theuerkauf's rig.
cards.reverse: The finding that the center body absorbed far more thermal energy than the outer channel wall because combustion products strike the convergent inner surface more directly.

cards.answer: Heat flux is far from uniform along the channel length: the base near the injection plane can see about four times the wall-average flux, falling off sharply toward the exit. It means a single embedded thermocouple samples a local flux, not a representative wall average.
cards.hook: Imagine standing near a blowtorch's nozzle versus ten feet down the pipe. The heat hits hardest right where the flame starts and tapers off fast, about four times average near injection versus the tail.
cards.reverse: The pattern where thermal loading is much higher near the injection end of the channel and drops sharply toward the exit, meaning one sensor location cannot stand in for the whole wall.

cards.answer: A resistance temperature detector on Kapton tape sampling surface temperature at up to 1 megahertz, used on the uncooled companion rig to catch detonation-frequency heat pulses. Its surface temperature history is converted to instantaneous flux through a semi-infinite solid model.
cards.hook: A strip of Kapton tape with a resistance thermometer glued on, fast enough to catch a detonation wave slapping the wall a million times a second, like a seismograph for heat pulses.
cards.reverse: A fast surface temperature sensor built from a resistance element on tape, used to catch the rapid heat spikes on an uncooled test rig.

cards.answer: Turning a fast surface temperature record into instantaneous heat flux using the closed-form solution for a wall that behaves as infinitely deep over short times. It is how the RTD signal becomes a flux time-history without instrumenting the back face.
cards.hook: Treat the wall like it goes down forever, so a quick heat pulse never feels the back side. That lets you back out flux from a surface temperature trace with one clean formula, no back face sensor needed.
cards.reverse: The method of converting a fast surface temperature history into a heat flux time series by assuming the wall behaves as infinitely deep over the short timescale involved.

cards.answer: The near-constant surface temperature an uncooled wall settles to within a short run once heat in roughly balances conduction away, about 250 degrees Celsius at one axial station in Theuerkauf's uncooled runs. It is what makes a single equilibrium reading usable for back-calculating h.
cards.hook: Like a pot on low simmer that stops climbing once heat in matches heat leaking out, the uncooled wall settled near 250 C and stayed there, a stable plateau you can actually use for a calculation.
cards.reverse: The roughly constant surface temperature an uncooled wall reaches within a short run once incoming heat balances conduction losses, useful for solving for h at a single equilibrium point.

cards.answer: Measured wave speeds above 3000 meters per second exceeded the H2/air CJ value near 1960 meters per second, hinting at preheating, elevated inlet conditions, or multiple simultaneous waves. It is a reminder to measure wave count directly before comparing heat pulse data to single-wave theory.
cards.hook: Theory says H2 and air detonation should cruise near 1960 m/s, but Theuerkauf clocked speeds over 3000, like a car doing 90 in a 55, a red flag that preheat or extra waves are in play.
cards.reverse: The mismatch where measured propagation speeds far exceeded the theoretical ideal self sustaining detonation speed for H2 and air, pointing to preheating or several waves running at once.

cards.answer: More than one detonation wave traveling around the annulus at once, which doubles or triples the apparent frequency and speed inferred from a fixed sensor. The calorimetric LHV numbers still hold, but the instantaneous flux timing does not match a single-wave assumption.
cards.hook: Picture two or three detonation fronts chasing each other around the same racetrack instead of one lap at a time. A fixed sensor sees hits that look twice or three times too fast if you assume just one wave.
cards.reverse: The situation where more than one detonation front circles the annulus at once, inflating the apparent wave frequency a single sensor would infer.

cards.answer: The uncooled RTD gauges degraded quickly, so Theuerkauf drew no firm steady-state conclusions from them; the 250 degree and peak flux figures are early-time transients. It flags that sensor durability, not just accuracy, limits long uncooled measurements.
cards.hook: The Kapton gauges basically cook themselves recording the data, like a thermometer that burns up before it finishes the reading, so the 250 C and peak flux numbers are early snapshots, not a verdict on steady state.
cards.reverse: The problem where the fast uncooled sensors degraded too quickly for the researchers to trust any conclusion about long run, steady thermal behavior.

cards.answer: The base can spike above 9 megawatts per square meter for an instant as the wave passes, while the cycle-average and wall-average flux are far lower, in the range below one megawatt per square meter. Confusing the two overstates the steady thermal load by an order of magnitude.
cards.hook: A lightning strike versus the average weather: the base briefly spikes past 9 megawatts per square meter as the wave passes, but the cycle average sits under 1 megawatt. Mixing the two overstates the real load tenfold.
cards.reverse: The distinction between the momentary flux spike as the detonation front sweeps by and the much lower flux averaged over a full cycle or the whole wall.

cards.answer: The rig wall is 316 stainless with conductivity near 16 watts per meter kelvin and diffusivity near 4 times ten to the minus six square meters per second at operating temperature. These set how fast the wall responds and are the inputs any flux inversion needs.
cards.hook: 316 stainless, the same alloy as a kitchen sink, conducts heat sluggishly at about 16 W per meter kelvin and diffuses a temperature pulse slowly, the numbers any flux back calculation must plug in.
cards.reverse: The conductivity and diffusivity values of the stainless steel test rig material that any inverse heat flux calculation from thermocouple data depends on.

cards.prompt: Weak equivalence ratio effect on percent LHV
cards.answer: Theuerkauf found equivalence ratio had little effect on heat absorption as a fraction of LHV, so the LHV-normalized framework transfers across Phi. Absolute flux still scales with total fuel flow, so this does not mean flux itself is insensitive to Phi.
cards.hook: Turning the fuel rich or fuel lean knob barely moves the needle on what fraction of total energy the wall steals, so the percent LHV yardstick holds no matter where phi sits, even though raw flux still tracks total fuel flow.
cards.reverse: The result that varying the fuel to oxidizer ratio made little difference to the wall's heat absorption expressed as a share of the propellant's total chemical energy.

cards.answer: The tests ran near ambient chamber pressure, while engines like HADES run around 10 bar. Since convective flux scales roughly with mass flux and pressure, Theuerkauf's raw heat flux numbers are a lower bound, not a ceiling, for a pressurized engine.
cards.hook: Theuerkauf tested at sea level pressure while an engine like HADES runs around 10 bar, like judging a fire hose by a garden hose test. Since flux scales with pressure, the low pressure numbers are a floor, not a ceiling.
cards.reverse: The warning that because these tests ran near ambient pressure rather than an engine's actual elevated operating pressure, the measured heat flux values should be read as a minimum, not the real expected load.

cards.answer: A combustor where a detonation wave (a supersonic combustion front locked to a shock) travels continuously around an annular channel instead of combustion happening as a steady deflagration. The wave recirculates at kilohertz frequencies, so any point on the wall sees a periodic, not steady, heat load.
cards.hook: Picture a sparkler swung in a fast circle so the flame never goes out, that unbroken ring of fire is the detonation wave chasing itself around the annulus.
cards.reverse: A combustor design where a shock locked supersonic flame front loops endlessly around a ring shaped channel, so any given spot on the wall only feels short pulses of heat, not a steady load.

cards.answer: A CFD approach that time-resolves large-scale unsteady flow features, like a detonation wave passing, while still modeling turbulence statistically rather than resolving every eddy. It is expensive enough that this paper could only afford 2 to 3 detonation cycles before stopping the simulation.
cards.hook: Think of it as a strobe camera on the flow, catching the big flash of the wave passing while blurring the fine turbulent grain into an average.
cards.reverse: The CFD approach that time resolves a large unsteady event like a passing detonation front while still modeling the small scale turbulence statistically, so costly here it only bought a couple of cycles.

cards.answer: An OpenFOAM solver that marches a diffusion-type equation (here, transient heat conduction) forward in time on a mesh. The paper uses it to solve for temperature inside the solid wall once a boundary heat load is supplied from outside.
cards.hook: A solver named for Laplace, quietly letting heat seep through solid metal like syrup soaking into a sponge, no gas physics involved at all.
cards.reverse: The OpenFOAM tool used to march transient conduction through the solid wall once an outside heat load is fed in as a boundary input.

cards.answer: A material property, D_T = k/(rho times specific heat), that measures how fast a temperature disturbance spreads through a solid by conduction, independent of how much total heat it takes. A higher value means the wall's interior responds to a surface heat pulse more quickly.
cards.hook: Alpha is how fast a shout travels through a crowded room, not how loud the room gets, it is about speed of spreading, not total heat stored.
cards.reverse: A material property built from conductivity over density times specific heat that tells you how quickly a temperature swing propagates inward through a solid.

cards.prompt: Convective heat transfer coefficient (htc or h)
cards.answer: The proportionality constant in q double-prime = h times (T_gas minus T_wall), converting a temperature difference between hot gas and a wall into a heat flux. It is not a fundamental constant: it depends on flow conditions and, critically, on what reference gas temperature it is paired with, so h values from different papers are only comparable if you check the reference temperature too.
cards.hook: Little h is a currency exchange rate between a temperature gap and a heat flux, and like any exchange rate it is meaningless until you know which two references it is converting.
cards.reverse: The proportionality number linking a gas to wall temperature difference to surface heat flux, comparable across papers only once you check which reference gas temperature it is paired with.

cards.answer: A boundary condition that specifies the gradient of temperature at a surface, here set by dT/dn = h(T_gas minus T_wall)/k, rather than fixing the temperature itself (a Dirichlet condition). It lets the wall's surface temperature float and respond to the imposed heat flux over time.
cards.hook: This one cares about slope, not height, picture setting the steepness of a ramp at the wall instead of nailing a fixed floor level.
cards.reverse: A wall condition that pins the temperature gradient at the surface through the convective flux relation, letting the actual surface temperature drift freely in response.

cards.answer: An OpenFOAM feature letting a user compile custom C++ logic directly into a boundary condition, used here to make the convective heat flux vary in both space (around the annulus) and time (as the detonation wave passes) instead of being a single fixed value.
cards.hook: Picture stapling a snippet of your own C++ onto the boundary face so the flux can dance through space and time instead of sitting frozen at one number.
cards.reverse: An OpenFOAM feature that lets custom compiled logic drive a boundary, so the heat load can vary both around the ring and through the detonation cycle.

cards.answer: A simplification where hot gas temperatures from the CFD drive the wall's heat conduction, but the wall's temperature is never fed back to change the gas simulation. It is justified when gas temperature is much higher than wall temperature and the run time is short enough that wall heating cannot meaningfully cool the gas back.
cards.hook: A one way mirror between gas and wall, the gas's heat shines through into the metal, but the wall's warmth never shines back to change the gas.
cards.reverse: A modeling shortcut where hot gas temperatures push heat into the solid but the solid's own temperature is never fed back to alter the gas field.

cards.answer: The point where the leading detonation shock, a trailing oblique shock, and a shear layer all intersect, a classic feature of real detonation fronts. This paper finds the wall's hottest spot sits downstream of the detonation zone near this triple-point region, not right at the front itself.
cards.hook: Three lines crossing at one spot, leading shock, trailing oblique shock, and shear layer, and that crossroads is quietly where the wall gets hottest.
cards.reverse: The intersection of the main detonation shock, a secondary oblique shock, and a shear layer, found here to sit downstream of the front near the wall's peak heating.

cards.answer: The region just ahead of the detonation wave where fresh, cooler reactants are being injected and have not yet burned. It offers the wall some natural, regenerative-style cooling, but in this non-premixed geometry that cooling only reaches about 40 percent of the detonation height.
cards.hook: The cool trailing edge of a comet, fresh unburned gas streaming in right behind the fireball, giving the wall a brief breather before the next lap.
cards.reverse: The stretch just ahead of the traveling detonation front where cool fresh reactants have entered but not yet ignited, giving the wall partial natural cooling.

cards.answer: The theoretical post-detonation gas temperature predicted for a self-sustaining detonation wave, used as a fixed reference temperature in some heat-flux models. The paper flags that comparing an h value referenced to T_CJ against an h value referenced to local instantaneous CFD gas temperature is comparing two different definitions, not two competing measurements.
cards.hook: A textbook thermostat setting, a fixed theoretical peak baked into idealized detonation math, not the flickering number a live CFD cell actually reads.
cards.reverse: The idealized post wave gas temperature predicted for a self sustaining detonation, one fixed reference point that must not be confused with a local instantaneous CFD reading.

cards.answer: Roughly the distance, given by the square root of (diffusivity divided by pi times frequency), that a periodic surface temperature swing can propagate into a solid before it is damped out. At the roughly 3 kHz detonation frequency in steel this depth is only about 20 micrometers, which is why thermocouples buried a few millimeters deep see just the cycle-averaged flux, not the fast oscillation.
cards.hook: A fast drumbeat on a metal sheet barely dents below the surface, only a slow bass note reaches deep into the plate, that shallow skin the fast beat reaches is this depth.
cards.reverse: The rough distance a periodic surface temperature ripple can push into a solid before fading out, only tens of micrometers deep at kilohertz detonation frequencies in steel.

cards.answer: An empirical formula relating a dimensionless heat transfer number (the Nusselt number) to flow parameters like Reynolds and Prandtl number, normally used to predict a convective coefficient without full CFD. The cited prior work tried this approach for the RDC and found it did not work, which is why this paper instead bounds htc empirically from measured heat flux data.
cards.hook: A cookbook formula promising to bake a heat transfer coefficient out of flow numbers alone, except this recipe burned when tried on a rotating detonation combustor.
cards.reverse: An empirical equation tying a dimensionless heat transfer number to flow parameters like Reynolds and Prandtl, tried and found not to work here, prompting an empirical bound instead.

cards.answer: A simulation approach that solves the fluid flow and the solid conduction together as one coupled system, so each side continuously affects the other. This paper deliberately avoids that expensive full coupling by instead running the wall conduction as a separate, cheaper model driven one-way by an already-computed gas temperature field.
cards.hook: Picture gas and metal locked in a real conversation, each sentence from one side changing what the other says next, a full back and forth instead of a lecture.
cards.reverse: A fully coupled simulation approach solving fluid flow and solid conduction together so each continuously influences the other, deliberately skipped here for a cheaper one way model.

cards.answer: A boundary condition of zero heat flux, meaning no heat crosses that surface at all. The paper applies this to the wall's outer surface and end faces as a simplification, which tends to over-predict wall temperature somewhat since it ignores real external cooling losses.
cards.hook: Wrap the outer wall in an imaginary thermos, sealed so tight that not a single watt of heat sneaks across it.
cards.reverse: A zero heat flux surface condition applied to the outer wall and end faces, a simplification that tends to overstate wall temperature by ignoring real external losses.

cards.answer: You know the temperature history at a point inside a solid, and you want to work backward to find the temperature and heat flux at a surface you cannot instrument, usually because it is too hot or otherwise inaccessible. It is called inverse because normally you'd solve forward from a known boundary condition to get temperatures inside; here you flip that direction, and flipping it is what makes the math touchy.
cards.hook: Picture running a movie of heat spreading through a wall in reverse, like rewinding a splashed water balloon back into a solid ball, the further back you rewind the shakier the picture gets.
cards.reverse: You only have temperature readings from a point buried inside a wall and must work backward to the surface conditions you could never directly measure, a calculation that flipping direction makes numerically touchy. Name this class of problem.

cards.answer: The ordinary, well-behaved half of the method: given the measured temperatures at two known points inside a wall, solve the standard forward conduction equation for everything in between them. It is called direct because both boundaries have real data, so it is stable and easy compared to the inverse step that follows it.
cards.hook: Think of a DHCP network address handing itself out automatically, this is the easy, well-behaved half of the method, solving straight ahead between two known points with real data on both ends.
cards.reverse: Given two known temperature measurements inside a wall, solving the ordinary forward conduction equation for everything between them, stable and simple compared to the risky step that follows it. Name this problem.

cards.prompt: Space marching (as used in the Carasso S6 scheme)
cards.answer: Instead of stepping forward in time the way you normally solve a heat equation, this method steps forward in space: it builds the complete time history of temperature and flux at one spatial node, then uses that whole history to compute the next node out, one node at a time until it reaches the surface. It works this way because there is no boundary data at the hot surface to time-march toward, so space becomes the direction you march.
cards.hook: Picture walking down a dark hallway one room at a time, but in each room you first work out everything that happens there across all time before stepping into the next room, distance is the direction you walk, not the clock.
cards.reverse: A technique that builds the complete time history at one interior location before advancing outward to the next location, used because there is no known data at the hot surface to step forward through time toward. Name this marching strategy.

cards.answer: An inverse heat conduction solution is ill-posed because tiny errors or noise in the input data get amplified as the solution marches away from the measurement point, sometimes growing so fast that the answer becomes meaningless oscillation instead of a real signal. It is not a bug in the numerical scheme, it is a property of the underlying math: reversing diffusion is inherently unstable, so every practical method has to fight that amplification somehow.
cards.hook: Picture whispering one word into a hallway lined with megaphones, by the far end the mumble has become a deafening roar, that runaway amplification is baked into the physics itself, not a coding mistake.
cards.reverse: The underlying property that makes a reversed diffusion calculation inherently unstable, where small noise in the input snowballs into meaningless oscillation the farther the march travels from the data. Name this property.

cards.answer: A way of discretizing the conduction equation by dividing the domain into small control volumes centered on mesh nodes and writing an energy balance for each volume, rather than approximating derivatives directly at a point the way finite differences do. Vertex-based just means the nodes sit at the volume centers you actually track, which is convenient here because the two thermocouple locations become mesh nodes with known temperature directly.
cards.hook: Picture slicing a loaf of bread into chunks centered on the two spots where you actually have a thermocouple, then writing a budget for each chunk instead of trying to take a derivative at one knife-edge point.
cards.reverse: An approach to discretizing the conduction equation using small control volumes centered on the actual measurement locations, tracking an energy balance chunk by chunk rather than approximating a derivative pointwise. Name this discretization method.

cards.answer: A way of stepping a PDE forward in time that averages the spatial terms from the current time step and the next one, instead of using only one or the other. It buys second-order accuracy in time and unconditional stability for the diffusion equation, at the cost of needing an implicit solve at every step because the unknown future temperature appears on both sides of the equation.
cards.hook: Crank Nicolson sounds like cranking a handle exactly halfway, the scheme literally splits the difference, averaging the present and next time step fifty fifty for extra accuracy and stability.
cards.reverse: A time-stepping approach that averages the spatial terms of the current step and the upcoming one, buying second order accuracy and unconditional stability at the cost of an implicit solve every step. Name this scheme.

cards.answer: Truncation error is the mathematical gap between the exact PDE and the discrete approximation you actually solve, usually expressed as how it shrinks with step size, like O(dt squared) or O(dx). Amplification factor describes something different but related: how much a small error already present in the solution grows or shrinks each time you take another step, which is the number that actually decides whether an ill-posed inverse scheme stays usable or blows up.
cards.hook: Two different report cards for the same math, one grades how far your formula strays from the true equation like a typo, the other grades how loudly an existing mistake gets shouted at the next step like a game of telephone.
cards.reverse: Distinguish the gap between a discrete formula and the exact PDE, which shrinks as step size shrinks, from the separate measure of how much an existing error grows or shrinks each step forward, the number that decides whether an unstable scheme survives. Name this pair of concepts.

cards.answer: A material property equal to conductivity divided by the product of density and specific heat, alpha equals k over rho times cp, and it measures how fast a temperature disturbance spreads through a material rather than how much heat it can carry. It is the single number that governs the diffusion equation once you strip away the flux terms, so it controls how quickly a surface heating event shows up at a thermocouple buried some distance away.
cards.hook: Picture alpha as the speed limit sign for heat, conductivity divided by density times heat capacity, telling you how fast a temperature bulletin spreads through a material rather than how much heat it can hold.
cards.reverse: The material property, conductivity over density times specific heat, that controls how quickly a surface heating event shows up at a sensor buried some distance inside the solid. Name this property.

cards.prompt: Half-cell energy balance (for extracting heat flux at a thermocouple)
cards.answer: To get the heat flux passing through a node where you only have temperature data, you write a conservation of energy statement over just half of the control volume next to that node, balancing conduction in against the rate of temperature rise stored in that half-cell. This is how the method converts a temperature-only measurement into a flux value without ever measuring flux directly.
cards.hook: Picture slicing a control volume clean in half right at the thermocouple, then doing bookkeeping only on the half you can see, conduction coming in weighed against the heat storing up as temperature rise there.
cards.reverse: A way of pulling a heat flux value out of a node using only temperature data, by writing conservation of energy over just one half of that node's control volume. Name this technique.

cards.answer: When an implicit scheme like Crank-Nicolson only couples each node to its immediate left and right neighbors, the resulting system of equations has a banded, tridiagonal structure, and the Thomas algorithm solves that structure in a single fast forward-and-back sweep instead of a full general matrix inversion. It is why an implicit 1-D conduction solve stays cheap even though it looks more complicated than an explicit one.
cards.hook: Picture a conga line where everyone only holds hands with the person immediately left and right, never reaching across the room, that neighbor only coupling lets the whole line be solved in one fast sweep out and back.
cards.reverse: The fast forward then back sweep for solving a banded system where each unknown couples only to its immediate neighbors, what keeps an implicit one dimensional solve cheap despite the added complexity. Name this algorithm.

cards.prompt: Sensitivity to thermocouple depth error vs. data error
cards.answer: This paper distinguishes two very different failure modes: get the physical depth of a thermocouple slightly wrong, and the surface answer is off by about the same proportion, a mild, predictable error. Get the temperature reading itself slightly wrong, and the surface heat flux error comes out worse than the input error and keeps growing over time, because a biased reading behaves like continuously adding or removing heat rather than just shifting a number.
cards.hook: Two ways to sabotage a measurement, drill the sensor a millimeter off and you get a mild, proportional dent in the answer, but nudge the temperature reading itself and the error compounds worse over time, like a rumor snowballing rather than a ruler that is just a bit short.
cards.reverse: The contrast between a small mistake in physical sensor placement, which produces a roughly proportional output error, and a small mistake in the reading itself, which produces a worse than proportional, growing error because it acts like continuously adding or removing heat. Name what is being compared.

cards.answer: Once you know the surface temperature and surface heat flux from the inverse solution, you can back out a single convective heat transfer coefficient describing the hot gas side by dividing the flux by the difference between the driving gas temperature and the wall surface temperature. It is a convenient way to summarize a whole time history of coupled T and q data as one physically meaningful number, as long as that number stays roughly constant over the event.
cards.hook: Once surface temperature and flux come out of the inverse solve, boil the whole jagged time history down to one summary number, like averaging a stock's rollercoaster week into a single closing price, by dividing flux by the gas to wall temperature gap.
cards.reverse: The single convective coefficient recovered once surface temperature and heat flux are both known, found by dividing the flux by the difference between the driving gas temperature and the wall's surface temperature. Name this quantity.

cards.prompt: Convergence test via mesh refinement (variance criterion)
cards.answer: To prove a numerical grid is fine enough, you keep cutting the spatial step in half and rerun the solution, then check whether the temperature at a chosen node stops changing meaningfully between refinements. Here that is done with a variance threshold: once the difference between successive refinements at the midpoint node is smaller than a chosen tolerance, the mesh is called converged and you stop refining.
cards.hook: Picture halving a photo's pixel size again and again until further halving stops changing what you see, the grid version keeps cutting the spatial step until the answer at your chosen node quits moving.
cards.reverse: The check that repeatedly halves the spatial step and compares the result at a chosen node, declaring the grid fine enough once successive refinements change by less than a set tolerance. Name this test.

cards.prompt: Stabilizing an ill-posed inverse scheme by coarsening the time step
cards.answer: One crude way to suppress the runaway oscillations of an ill-posed inverse march is simply to use a larger time step, which smooths out the high-frequency noise that would otherwise get amplified node by node. It is not true regularization in the formal mathematical sense, it is closer to a side effect: coarser time resolution throws away exactly the high-frequency content that ill-posedness likes to amplify, but it only works if your physical problem can tolerate that coarse a time step.
cards.hook: Picture deliberately using a blurrier camera to kill sensor noise, taking bigger time steps smooths away the jittery high frequency noise an unstable backward march would otherwise blow up, a side effect fix rather than a designed one.
cards.reverse: The crude trick of using a larger time step to tame the runaway oscillations of an unstable backward marching solution, working by discarding the high frequency content that the instability likes to amplify rather than through formal regularization. Name this practice.

cards.prompt: Inverse domain (in a two-thermocouple IHCP setup)
cards.answer: The inverse domain is the stretch of material between the thermocouple closest to the hot surface and that hot surface itself, the region where you have no direct measurement and must march the ill-posed solution. Making this stretch shorter, by drilling the near thermocouple as close to the hot face as practical, is one of the main levers for keeping error amplification under control.
cards.hook: Picture the last unlit stretch of hallway between your nearest working flashlight and the hot door you cannot open, that gap with zero direct light is exactly the risky territory you have to march through blind.
cards.reverse: The unmeasured stretch of material between the sensor closest to the hot face and that hot surface itself, the zone with no direct data where the unstable march has to travel. Name this region.

cards.prompt: Heat flux being lower order accurate than temperature
cards.answer: Because heat flux is proportional to a spatial derivative of temperature, computing it always costs one order of accuracy compared to computing temperature itself on the same discretized data. Practically this means you should always expect the flux estimate, and anything derived from it like an effective heat transfer coefficient, to carry noticeably more error than the temperature estimate it came from.
cards.hook: Picture temperature as a smooth hiking trail and flux as its slope, judging a slope from a bumpy trail is always noisier than just reading your altitude, so anything built from that slope inherits the extra shakiness.
cards.reverse: The general rule that a quantity depending on a spatial derivative of temperature always carries more numerical error than the temperature values it was computed from on the same grid. Name this rule.

cards.answer: A combustor where one or more detonation waves spin continuously around an annular channel, consuming fresh premixed reactant injected just behind each passing wave. Instead of burning at roughly constant pressure like a normal combustor, it produces a net pressure gain across the device, which is the whole reason people want to build one.
cards.hook: Picture a spinning pinwheel that never stops firing: RDC is a combustor where the flame chases its own tail around a ring, gulping fresh gas the instant it passes.
cards.reverse: An engine design where the burn travels endlessly around a ring shaped chamber and boosts pressure instead of just holding it steady, what is this device called?

cards.answer: The state reached just behind a detonation wave when the burned gas accelerates until it is moving exactly at the local speed of sound relative to the wave. It is the minimum-speed, self-sustaining detonation solution, and RDC analyses use CJ pressure, temperature, and velocity as the reference jump condition across the front.
cards.hook: CJ is the exit speed limit: burned gas leaves the wave at exactly Mach 1 relative to it, like a car merging onto a highway at the precise speed of traffic, no faster, no slower.
cards.reverse: The named condition where the products behind a detonation front move away at exactly the local sound speed, marking the slowest a wave can go and still sustain itself, what is it called?

cards.answer: The point where three flow features intersect: the detonation front itself, the oblique shock it trails into, and the slip line that separates burned gas from shock-processed unburned gas. In this paper it is the location of the peak time-averaged wall heat flux, even though the single highest instantaneous flux occurs right at the detonation front.
cards.hook: Three lines crossing on a map: the shock, the oblique shock it kicks off, and the slip line between burned and unburned gas all meet at one X mark, and that X is where the wall gets cooked hardest on average.
cards.reverse: Where a detonation front, the oblique shock trailing it, and the shear line between burned and unburned gas all meet, and where the highest time-averaged wall heating actually lands, what is that intersection point named?

cards.answer: A way of solving supersonic flow fields by tracing special lines, called characteristics, along which the governing partial differential equations collapse into simpler ordinary ones. Marching along these lines from known boundary data builds the whole 2D flow field point by point, without needing a full CFD mesh or solver.
cards.hook: Think of tracing invisible rail lines through a supersonic flow: follow them and the messy PDEs turn into simple ODEs you can walk along by hand.
cards.reverse: A technique for mapping a supersonic flow field by following special curves where the governing equations simplify, avoiding a full mesh solver entirely, what is this method called?

cards.answer: A deliberately simplified model that trades some physical fidelity for a huge speed advantage over full simulation. In this paper the ROM pairs a MOC flow field with a boundary-layer heat-transfer correlation, running in about 200 seconds on a laptop versus roughly 400 hours of 3D URANS, while staying within about 16 percent locally and 4 to 6 percent in bulk flux.
cards.hook: A laptop doing in 200 seconds what a supercomputer takes 400 hours to do, trading a sliver of accuracy for a thousand-fold speedup, like a sketch artist versus a photorealist painter.
cards.reverse: A deliberately stripped-down model that sacrifices some accuracy for a massive speed gain over full simulation, what is this general category of model called?

cards.answer: A CFD approach that still models turbulence statistically with a closure model, like k-omega SST, but keeps the time dependence in the equations so large-scale unsteady motion is directly resolved rather than averaged away. Here it lets the periodic rotation of the detonation wave be captured without paying for full DNS or LES cost.
cards.hook: Imagine filming in slow motion instead of a single long-exposure blur: URANS keeps the clock ticking on big unsteady events while still statistically smoothing the small turbulent chaos.
cards.reverse: A CFD method that resolves large-scale unsteady motion over time while still averaging turbulence statistically through a closure model, what is this approach called?

cards.answer: A 1957 NACA method for predicting the Stanton number of a turbulent, compressible boundary layer from a momentum-integral equation. This paper uses it as the heat-transfer engine of its ROM, converting the MOC-computed flow history into a wall heat flux, with one empirically calibrated shape-factor constant tuning the fit.
cards.hook: Two NACA researchers named Reshotko and Tucker handed engineers a 1957 shortcut formula, a cheat sheet for guessing how hot a boundary layer runs without solving the whole flow.
cards.reverse: A 1957 NACA formula that predicts a dimensionless heat-transfer number for a compressible turbulent boundary layer from a momentum-integral approach, what is this correlation called?

cards.answer: A dimensionless heat-transfer coefficient equal to the convective heat-transfer coefficient divided by the mass flux and specific heat of the flow, St = h over (rho times u times cp). It is the quantity the Reshotko-Tucker correlation actually predicts, and the paper converts it to wall heat flux rather than ever solving explicitly for h.
cards.hook: Think of a Stanton pass in football, quick and normalized: Stanton number is heat transfer normalized against the flow's own carrying capacity, no wasted motion.
cards.reverse: The dimensionless heat-transfer coefficient equal to h divided by mass flux times specific heat, what is this number called?

cards.answer: A single number that summarizes how much momentum a boundary layer has lost to friction against the wall, found by integrating the velocity deficit across the layer's thickness. The paper uses a Stewartson-transformed version so a compressible boundary layer can be handled with the same integral math built for incompressible flow.
cards.hook: Imagine the boundary layer as a crowd shuffling past a wall, losing momentum to friction with every step; squeeze that whole shuffle into one number and you get theta.
cards.reverse: A single integrated value summarizing how much velocity a boundary layer has lost to wall friction, transformed for compressible flow, what is this quantity called?

cards.answer: A ratio describing how full or how thin a boundary layer's velocity profile is, essentially displacement thickness divided by momentum thickness. The paper's flat-plate value, Hi,fp equal to 1.7, is the single constant tuned against a 2D validation case, and it is the one hand-calibrated knob in the entire correlation.
cards.hook: Picture two boundary layer profiles side by side, a fat lazy one and a thin sharp one; the ratio between them, displacement over momentum thickness, is the shape factor, 1.7 for a flat plate, the paper's one hand-tuned dial.
cards.reverse: A ratio of displacement thickness to momentum thickness describing how full or thin a boundary layer's velocity profile is, with 1.7 as the flat-plate value tuned in this paper, what is this ratio called?

cards.answer: A technique for handling compressible boundary-layer heat transfer by evaluating fluid properties at a fictitious reference enthalpy that sits between the wall and freestream values instead of at freestream conditions alone. Doing this lets an otherwise incompressible correlation stay reasonably accurate once viscous heating near the wall matters.
cards.hook: Named after Ernst Eckert, this trick evaluates gas properties at a made-up in-between enthalpy, splitting the difference between wall and freestream like averaging two temperatures on a thermostat.
cards.reverse: A technique that evaluates fluid properties at a fictitious enthalpy between wall and freestream conditions to keep an incompressible correlation accurate under compressible heating, what is this method called?

cards.answer: The enthalpy the wall would settle to if it exchanged no heat with the flow at all, driven purely by viscous dissipation converting some of the flow's kinetic energy back into heat right at the surface. The gap between this hypothetical value and the real wall enthalpy is what actually drives the computed wall heat flux.
cards.hook: Rub your hands together fast and they warm up with no outside heat source, that friction-only warmth is the adiabatic wall enthalpy: what the surface would reach from viscous dissipation alone.
cards.reverse: The hypothetical enthalpy a wall would reach with zero net heat exchange, driven purely by viscous dissipation near the surface, what is this quantity called?

cards.answer: A dimensionless measure of how far the first grid cell's center sits from a solid wall in turbulent CFD, expressed in units of local viscous length scale rather than physical distance. A y+ well below 1, as targeted in this paper's 3D mesh, means the viscous sublayer is directly resolved rather than approximated by a wall-function shortcut.
cards.hook: Y-plus under 1 means your first grid cell is basically pressed flush against the wall, close enough to feel the viscous sublayer directly instead of guessing at it with a wall function.
cards.reverse: A dimensionless distance measuring how far the nearest grid cell sits from a wall in viscous length units, used to judge whether the sublayer is directly resolved, what is this parameter called?

cards.answer: A way to estimate both the true, grid-independent answer and the numerical error of a CFD result by comparing solutions computed on two or more meshes of different refinement and extrapolating the trend toward infinitely fine resolution. This paper reports an extrapolated discretization error of about 10.95 percent on its average bottom-plate heat flux, even for the mesh it calls converged.
cards.hook: Lewis Fry Richardson's old trick: run the same problem on a coarse grid and a fine grid, then extrapolate the trend line off the edge of the paper to guess the answer at infinitely fine resolution.
cards.reverse: A method that estimates the true grid-independent result and its numerical error by comparing solutions on meshes of different refinement and extrapolating the trend, what is this technique called?

cards.answer: A class of combustion where the working fluid exits the device at a higher stagnation pressure than it entered with, instead of losing stagnation pressure the way ordinary constant-pressure deflagration burning does. It is the actual performance payoff RDCs chase, and this paper reports a converged pressure gain of 0.72 for its baseline case.
cards.hook: Most flames are pickpockets, stealing a little stagnation pressure as they burn; pressure gain combustion is the rare flame that hands some back, netting 0.72 gain in this paper's baseline.
cards.reverse: A class of combustion where the exiting gas ends up at higher stagnation pressure than it entered with, unlike ordinary constant-pressure burning, what is this category called?

cards.answer: The Zeldovich, von Neumann, Doring picture of a detonation's internal structure, a thin shock jump immediately followed by a finite reaction zone that burns the mixture up to the CJ state. This paper uses Cantera plus the Shock and Detonation Toolbox to compute that jump, and feeds the result into the MOC solver as its starting condition.
cards.hook: Zeldovich, von Neumann, and Doring's shared last-name-initials model: a razor-thin shock spike immediately followed by a short burning tail down to the CJ state, like a whip crack followed by a slow fizzle.
cards.reverse: The named model describing a detonation's internal structure as a thin shock jump followed by a finite reaction zone burning down to the Chapman-Jouguet state, what is this model called?

cards.answer: A combustor where one or more detonation waves spin continuously around an annular channel, consuming fresh propellant as they pass so combustion happens through a supersonic detonation front rather than a slow deflagration flame. It is studied because the pressure gain from detonation can raise thermodynamic efficiency compared to constant pressure burners.
cards.hook: Picture a merry go round on fire, a shockwave chasing its own tail around a ring, relighting fresh propellant every lap instead of burning in one steady flame.
cards.reverse: Name for a combustor where a supersonic detonation wave laps continuously around a ring shaped channel, consuming fresh propellant with each pass instead of a steady flame.

cards.answer: The theoretical state just behind a detonation wave where the burned products move away from the wave at exactly the local speed of sound, giving the minimum stable detonation speed. Designers use the CJ pressure and CJ temperature as the reference peak values that the rest of the cycle decays down from.
cards.hook: Chapman and Jouguet as two bouncers letting the burned exhaust out the door at exactly walking pace, the local speed of sound, no faster, no slower.
cards.reverse: What is the reference detonation state where the burned products leave the wave moving at exactly the local speed of sound, setting the minimum stable wave speed.

cards.answer: A ratio comparing the actual fuel to air mixture in a combustor against the exact stoichiometric mixture that would consume all fuel and oxidizer with nothing left over. Phi equal to 1 is stoichiometric, Phi below 1 is fuel lean, and HADES runs at Phi of 0.9 to trade a slightly cooler flame for lower fuel use.
cards.hook: A tiny Greek letter Phi balanced on a scale, tipped just under 1 for HADES, a hair lean of perfectly matched fuel and air.
cards.reverse: What ratio compares the actual fuel to oxidizer mixture against the exact stoichiometric mixture, telling you how lean or rich a burn runs.

cards.answer: A characteristic length, in millimeters, describing the diamond shaped cellular pattern a detonation wave etches as it travels through a given fuel, oxidizer, and pressure combination. It is an empirical fingerprint of how readily a mixture detonates, and the combustion annulus is sized as a multiple of it so the wave has room to stabilize.
cards.hook: Soot on a foil left by a passing detonation, a fish scale pattern of diamonds, each diamond's width is lambda, the mixture's own fingerprint of how eager it is to detonate.
cards.reverse: What term names the width of the diamond pattern a detonation wave etches on a soot foil, an empirical measure of how readily a mixture detonates.

cards.answer: A NASA developed code that computes the equilibrium composition, temperature, and thermodynamic properties of combustion products for a chosen propellant pair and condition. HADES wraps CEA inside a MATLAB sizing script to generate the CJ temperatures and pressures that feed the rest of the design.
cards.hook: A NASA scientist stirring a pot of chemical equilibrium soup, spitting out exact flame temperature and pressure numbers to feed the sizing script.
cards.reverse: What NASA code computes the equilibrium composition, temperature, and pressure of combustion products for a chosen propellant pair and condition.

cards.answer: A correction factor, always less than one, that scales an ideal inviscid flow rate down to the real mass flow an orifice or slot actually passes, accounting for friction and flow contraction. It lets a designer size an injector opening to hit a target mass flow at a given pressure drop.
cards.hook: Water squeezing through a crimped garden hose nozzle, friction and contraction always steal a slice of flow, CD is the fraction that actually gets through, always under 1.
cards.reverse: What is the correction factor, always less than one, that scales an ideal inviscid flow rate down to the real mass flow an opening actually passes.

cards.answer: The pressure drop across an injector expressed as a percentage of the downstream chamber pressure. A high stiffness, such as the 100 percent used in HADES, means the drop is large enough to choke the injector so upstream feed pressure stays isolated from the pressure spikes of a passing detonation wave.
cards.hook: A stiff steel spring holding the upstream fuel feed rock solid while shockwaves hammer up from below, the pressure drop across it acting as a shock absorber.
cards.reverse: What term describes the pressure drop across a propellant injector, expressed as a percent of downstream chamber pressure, that isolates upstream feed from combustion pressure spikes.

cards.answer: A single number between 0 and 1 describing how uniformly fuel and air have blended at a given cross section, calculated from how much the local fuel fraction still varies relative to a fully unmixed state. A value near 1 means the mixture is essentially uniform by the time it reaches the detonation zone.
cards.hook: Pouring cream into coffee and swirling until no streak remains, a value near 1 means the fuel and air have fully blended before they reach the detonation zone.
cards.reverse: What number between zero and one describes how uniformly fuel and air have blended together at a given point in the flow.

cards.answer: A combustor with no active cooling passages, where the solid wall simply absorbs and stores the heat load for the duration of a short test instead of shedding it continuously. It survives only because the hotfire is brief enough that the wall does not reach a damaging temperature before the test ends.
cards.hook: A giant metal block acting like an ice cube dropped in a hot drink, no coolant plumbing at all, just soaking up the heat until the short test ends before it melts.
cards.reverse: What term names a combustor with no active cooling passages, where the solid wall simply absorbs the heat load for the length of a short test.

cards.answer: A low carbon grade of 316 stainless steel, chosen for its corrosion resistance, weldability, and retained strength at elevated temperature.
