HADES uses it as the uncooled chamber wall material because the low carbon content resists the intergranular cracking that welding can otherwise cause in stainless.
cards.hook: A welder avoiding cracked seams because the low carbon in this grade of stainless resists the intergranular cracking that welding usually causes.
cards.reverse: Which stainless steel grade, chosen for corrosion resistance and low carbon content that resists weld cracking, forms HADES's uncooled chamber wall.

cards.answer: A dimensionless group that compares the heat actually transferred into a wall to the heat the flow's own thermal capacity could carry, effectively normalizing convective heat flux. HADES computes a modified Stanton number, tuned with an empirical constant, to predict wall heat flux from local boundary layer conditions.
cards.hook: Thomas Stanton's dial comparing how much heat actually soaked into the wall against how much heat the flow could have carried away.
cards.reverse: What dimensionless group compares the heat actually transferred into a wall against the heat carrying capacity of the flow itself.

cards.prompt: Gas side heat transfer coefficient (effective h)
cards.answer: A single number, in watts per square meter kelvin, that lumps together all the complexity of convection from the hot detonation products into the wall so that heat flux equals h times the gas to wall temperature difference. HADES Phase 1 back calculates an effective h by matching a wall conduction model to temperatures actually measured by thermocouples during a hotfire.
cards.hook: A middleman letter h translating a temperature gap into a heat flow rate, its value read backward out of thermocouples buried in the wall during a hotfire.
cards.reverse: What single lumped value, in watts per square meter kelvin, converts a gas to wall temperature difference into heat flux, and gets back calculated from measured thermocouple data.

cards.answer: The ring shaped combustion passage between an inner and outer cylindrical wall where the detonation wave actually travels, as opposed to a single round bore chamber. Its gap width and diameter are sized directly from the detonation cell size so the wave has enough room to remain stable.
cards.hook: A donut shaped combustion channel, the ring gap between an inner and outer wall where the detonation wave actually laps around.
cards.reverse: What term names the ring shaped combustion passage between two cylindrical walls where the detonation wave travels, sized off the detonation cell size.

cards.prompt: Factor of safety (FoS) in fatigue analysis
cards.answer: The margin an engineering design carries above the minimum stress or cycle count needed to survive its intended life, reported as a ratio rather than an absolute number. A minimum FoS of 1.75 at a given number of cycles means the most highly loaded point in the structure still has extra margin beyond the bare minimum needed to survive that many cycles.
cards.hook: A tightrope walker carrying 1.75 times the strength she needs, still crossing the wire of cycles to failure with margin left in reserve.
cards.reverse: What ratio expresses the margin a structure carries above the minimum stress or cycle count required to survive its intended fatigue life.

cards.prompt: Phase 1 method: thermocouple back calculation (TCC1, TCC2)
cards.answer: The experimental approach where wall mounted thermocouples, typically labeled by channel such as TCC1 and TCC2, record the temperature rise during a hotfire, and an inverse conduction calculation works backward from those measured temperatures to infer the gas side film coefficient h that must have produced them. It turns a predicted design number into a value grounded in real test data.
cards.hook: Two buried sensors, TCC1 and TCC2, reporting a temperature climb during a hotfire, then the math runs backward like rewinding a film to reveal the invisible heat flux that caused it.
cards.reverse: What experimental approach uses wall mounted sensors and an inverse conduction calculation to infer the gas side film coefficient from a measured hotfire temperature rise.

cards.answer: The specific combination of mass flow, equivalence ratio, and chamber pressure at which an engine is actually run or analyzed, as distinct from the full range of conditions it could be operated across. HADES's nominal operating point is about 0.907 kilograms per second of air, Phi of 0.9, and roughly 10 bar chamber pressure.
cards.hook: A single dot pinned on a graph with three axes, mass flow, Phi, and pressure, HADES parks its dot at 0.907 kilograms per second, 0.9, and 10 bar.
cards.reverse: What term names the specific combination of mass flow, equivalence ratio, and chamber pressure at which an engine is actually run or analyzed.

cards.answer: A rocket combustor where one or more detonation waves race continuously around an annular channel instead of burning propellant in a steady deflagration. The spinning wave both mixes and ignites fresh propellant as it passes, so thrust comes from a repeating detonation cycle rather than a constant-pressure flame.
cards.hook: Picture a fiery pinwheel spinning nonstop inside a rocket throat, each spin lighting the next.
cards.reverse: A rocket combustor that keeps a shock driven combustion front looping around a ring shaped channel, so thrust comes from that repeating spin instead of one steady flame.

cards.answer: Shorthand for a rocket engine burning gaseous methane fuel with gaseous oxygen oxidizer. It is chosen over hydrogen/oxygen in Deimos because methane reacts more slowly, which lowers the risk of the fuel igniting too early inside the engine.
cards.hook: Methagox sounds like methane wearing oxygen goggles, paired up but slower to react than jumpy hydrogen.
cards.reverse: The gaseous fuel and gaseous oxidizer combination Deimos picked because its sluggish reaction speed cuts the odds of the fuel igniting too early inside the engine.

cards.answer: A single number describing how rich or lean a fuel/oxidizer mixture is, defined so that Phi equals one at the exact stoichiometric ratio, above one means excess fuel, and below one means excess oxidizer. Deimos runs its campaign across Phi from 0.5 to 2.0 with 1.3 chosen as the best tradeoff between performance and detonation stability.
cards.hook: Phi looks like a circle with a line through it, a dial you turn between too little and too much fuel, Deimos parks the needle at 1.3.
cards.reverse: The single number, swept from 0.5 to 2.0 with 1.3 as the chosen sweet spot, that says whether a fuel and oxidizer mixture runs rich or lean relative to the exact stoichiometric balance.

cards.answer: The characteristic diamond-shaped repeating pattern length that a detonation wave traces as it propagates, a property of the specific propellant mixture and pressure rather than of the engine hardware. Designers use lambda from reference databases to set the minimum channel gap and diameter a rotating detonation engine needs to sustain a stable wave.
cards.hook: Lambda traces diamond shaped scales, the same diamond pattern a detonation wave stamps as it slithers through the mixture.
cards.reverse: The mixture and pressure dependent diamond pattern length pulled from reference databases to set the minimum channel gap and diameter a rotating detonation engine needs.

cards.answer: The theoretical steady state of a detonation wave where the reacting gas behind the wave reaches exactly sonic speed relative to the wave front, giving the maximum self-sustaining detonation speed for a given mixture. Real rotating detonation engines typically run at only 60 to 90 percent of this predicted CJ velocity, so their actual pressure and temperature must be corrected downward from the ideal CJ values.
cards.hook: Think of CJ as the wave's posted speed limit, the fastest a self feeding detonation can steadily run, though Deimos usually drives 60 to 90 percent under it.
cards.reverse: The ideal, self sustaining state and top speed a detonation wave reaches when the burned gas trailing it moves exactly at the local speed of sound, a ceiling real engines only partly reach.

cards.answer: Two ways of aiming propellant jets so fuel and oxidizer mix by colliding: a doublet fires one fuel stream directly into one oxidizer stream, while a triplet fires two streams of one propellant converging onto a single central stream of the other. Deimos uses a swappable injector plate so the same engine can test both patterns and isolate how injector geometry affects the detonation.
cards.hook: A doublet is two streams colliding like a handshake, a triplet is three streams meeting like a tripod, and Deimos swaps the plate to test both.
cards.reverse: Two ways of aiming colliding propellant jets so they mix on impact, one stream firing into one versus two streams converging onto a single central stream.

cards.answer: A nozzle shape that replaces the enclosed bell of a conventional nozzle with an open, tapered spike, letting the surrounding atmospheric pressure form the outer boundary of the exhaust flow so the nozzle self-adjusts as altitude changes. Truncating the spike, cutting off its tip early such as at 40 percent of full length, trades a little ideal performance for a shorter, lighter, easier to cool nozzle.
cards.hook: Picture a nozzle turned inside out, a bare spike in open air letting the sky itself form the missing wall, then lop off the tip to save weight.
cards.reverse: An open, tapered exhaust shape that lets surrounding atmospheric pressure form its outer boundary so it self adjusts with altitude, often shortened near its tip to trade a little performance for less weight and easier cooling.

cards.answer: A standard NASA software tool that computes the equilibrium chemistry and thermodynamic properties of a combustion product mixture, given the propellants, mixture ratio, and chamber pressure. Deimos uses it to predict specific impulse and characteristic velocity, the baseline performance numbers that everything else in the sizing chain builds on.
cards.hook: CEA is the propellant fortune teller, feed it fuel, oxidizer, and pressure, it hands back the chemistry numbers everything else builds on.
cards.reverse: The standard NASA software Deimos runs to predict specific impulse and characteristic velocity from a chosen propellant pair, mixture ratio, and chamber pressure.

cards.answer: An unwanted condition where propellant ignites and burns steadily in the refill zone behind the detonation wave, instead of waiting to be consumed by the next passing wave. It wastes propellant and can eventually quench the detonation entirely, and it is the main reason Deimos favors methane over the faster-reacting, more flameholding-prone hydrogen.
cards.hook: Imagine a campfire that refuses to go out behind the wave, quietly eating fuel meant for the next lap until the whole detonation sputters dead.
cards.reverse: The failure mode where propellant starts burning steadily in the fresh charge zone instead of waiting for the next passing wave, wasting fuel and risking snuffing out the detonation entirely.

cards.answer: The time a fuel/oxidizer mixture takes to spontaneously ignite once it reaches a given temperature and pressure, without any external spark. Deimos compares this delay for methane and hydrogen using a Cantera chemical kinetics model, finding hydrogen ignites one to two orders of magnitude faster, which is why it carries higher flameholding risk.
cards.hook: A fuse that lights itself with no match, just heat and pressure and a countdown, hydrogen's fuse burns a hundred times shorter than methane's.
cards.reverse: The time a fuel and oxidizer mixture takes to spontaneously catch fire at a given temperature and pressure with no external spark, used here to show hydrogen igniting far faster than methane.

cards.answer: A model of compressible gas flow through a constant-area duct where friction is the only thing changing the gas properties along its length, no heat added or removed. Deimos applies it to size its long, narrow propellant injector orifices, since wall friction there is significant enough that a simple choked-orifice equation alone is not accurate.
cards.hook: Picture gas squeezing down a long, rough drinking straw, friction alone dragging its properties around with no heating or cooling in play.
cards.reverse: A compressible flow model for a constant area duct where wall friction alone changes the gas properties along its length, applied here to size long, narrow injector orifices.

cards.answer: A correction factor, less than one, applied to an ideal flow equation to account for real losses in an orifice, such as the flow contracting slightly after it passes through. Deimos uses Cd equals 0.60 in its compressible orifice equation to translate a required mass flow rate into the actual upstream manifold pressure needed.
cards.hook: Cd is the reality tax on an ideal flow formula, a 0.60 haircut for how a real jet pinches narrower just past the hole.
cards.reverse: A less than one correction factor applied to an ideal orifice flow equation to account for real losses like jet contraction, set to 0.60 when translating required mass flow into upstream manifold pressure.

cards.answer: The ratio of the pressure drop across an injector to the chamber pressure it feeds into, expressed as a percentage. A high stiffness value means the injector pressure drop dominates over chamber pressure fluctuations, which decouples the propellant feed from combustion instabilities and helps keep the flow steady.
cards.hook: A stiff injector refuses to flinch when chamber pressure jitters, because its own pressure drop is the bigger boss in the room.
cards.reverse: A percentage comparing the pressure drop across an injector to the chamber pressure it feeds, where a high value decouples propellant feed from combustion pressure fluctuations.

cards.prompt: Method of Characteristics (MOC), applied to aerospike design
cards.answer: A numerical technique for designing supersonic nozzle contours by tracing the paths, called characteristic lines, along which small pressure disturbances propagate through the flow. Deimos uses Angelino's axisymmetric version of this method to compute the aerospike's outer contour point by point, then cross-checks the result against a second, independent MOC formulation.
cards.hook: Follow the ripples, not the water, this method traces the invisible lines along which pressure disturbances travel to sketch the spike's outer curve point by point.
cards.reverse: A point by point supersonic contour design technique that traces disturbance propagation paths, used via an axisymmetric formulation and cross checked against a second, independent version.

cards.answer: A performance number that measures how effectively a combustion chamber converts propellant into hot, high-pressure gas, independent of the nozzle. It depends only on the propellant chemistry and chamber conditions, and Deimos gets its value from NASA CEA to compute mass flow rate and thrust.
cards.hook: c-star grades the combustion chamber alone on a report card, ignoring the nozzle entirely, chemistry and chamber conditions are the only inputs.
cards.reverse: A performance figure describing only how well a chamber converts propellant into hot, high pressure gas, independent of the nozzle, pulled from equilibrium chemistry software to compute mass flow and thrust.

cards.answer: A calculation method, adapted from Kuo's approach, that takes the fraction of ideal CJ velocity an engine actually achieves and outputs corrected pressure and temperature efficiency fractions for that real, sub-ideal detonation state. Deimos needs this because a wave running below CJ speed does not sit on the standard Rankine-Hugoniot curve that ideal detonation theory assumes.
cards.hook: Real waves limp in below the ideal pace, so this Kuo derived formula docks the pressure and temperature numbers down to match the slower, real world lap.
cards.reverse: A calculation, adapted from Kuo's approach, that converts the actual fraction of ideal detonation speed achieved into corrected pressure and temperature efficiency fractions, needed because a below ideal wave falls off the standard detonation jump curve.

cards.answer: A propulsion device that burns fuel and oxidizer inside a ring shaped channel using a continuously spinning detonation wave instead of a steady flame, so the surrounding metal hardware sees repeated, extremely hot, high pressure pulses rather than one constant condition.
cards.hook: Picture a flaming donut with a tornado racing endlessly around its ring, hammering the metal with pulse after pulse instead of one steady blaze.
cards.reverse: An engine design where fuel and air burn via a continuously circling shock driven wave inside an annular channel, subjecting the walls to repeated intense pulses rather than a steady flame.

cards.answer: A simulation step that tracks how temperature at every point in a part changes over time as heat moves in and out, used instead of a steady state assumption whenever a part never gets long enough to settle at one temperature, such as a wall during a short hotfire.
cards.hook: Think of a stopwatch glued to a thermometer, tracking the part's temperature frame by frame instead of waiting for it to settle.
cards.reverse: The simulation step that follows how temperature evolves over time at every location in a component, used when the part never reaches equilibrium.

cards.answer: A simulation step that takes a temperature field or other load frozen at one moment in time and computes the resulting stress and strain in the part, treating inertia and motion effects as negligible, which is why it can be chained directly after a thermal analysis result.
cards.hook: Imagine freezing a single film frame of the heat map and asking only how much does this squeeze the metal right now, ignoring any motion.
cards.reverse: The simulation step that converts a temperature snapshot or applied load at one instant into the resulting stress and strain, ignoring inertia.

cards.answer: A method for predicting how many repeated load cycles a part survives before cracking, built by splitting the total strain range into an elastic portion tied to stress amplitude and a plastic portion tied to strain amplitude, each fitted with its own material constants from cyclic test data.
cards.hook: Picture bending a paperclip: a springy elastic wobble plus a stubborn plastic kink, each tracked by its own curve to predict when it snaps.
cards.reverse: A cycles to failure prediction method that splits total strain into an elastic, stress driven piece and a plastic, strain driven piece, each calibrated from cyclic test data.

cards.answer: A fatigue damage measure calculated by multiplying the peak tensile stress reached in a cycle by that cycle's strain amplitude, chosen because it captures how a cycle that is more tensile than compressive does extra damage even when the strain swing itself looks moderate.
cards.hook: Picture multiplying the highest tug of the cycle by how far it stretched, penalizing a pull that is mostly tension instead of push pull.
cards.reverse: A damage metric formed by multiplying a cycle's peak tensile stress by its strain amplitude, so tension dominated cycles score as more harmful.

cards.answer: Stress amplitude is half the difference between the highest and lowest stress reached during one load cycle, and strain amplitude is the same idea applied to strain, and both are the basic inputs that every fatigue life curve is built from.
cards.hook: Picture the swing of a pendulum: measure only half the arc from the highest point to the lowest, that half swing is the amplitude.
cards.reverse: Half the spread between the maximum and minimum values reached during one load cycle, tracked separately for stress and for strain, and the building block of every fatigue curve.

cards.answer: Failure that happens after a relatively small number of load cycles, often in the hundreds to low thousands, because the loading is severe enough to push the material past yield into plastic strain on every cycle, unlike high cycle fatigue where stresses stay elastic and failure takes millions of cycles.
cards.hook: Picture a wire coat hanger bent hard back and forth past its yield point, snapping after just a few dozen bends rather than a million gentle flexes.
cards.reverse: Failure occurring after only hundreds to a few thousand load repetitions because the stress is severe enough to yield the material every time, unlike the elastic, million cycle regime.

cards.prompt: Factor of safety applied to life vs. applied to stress
cards.answer: Two different ways to build in margin that are easy to confuse: dividing the predicted number of cycles to failure by a knockdown number to get a smaller design life, versus asking how much higher the applied stress could go before failure at a fixed cycle count, and a shared numeric value between the two conventions does not mean they agree.
cards.hook: Picture two rulers measuring the same bridge: one counts how many trips before it cracks, the other counts how much heavier a truck could get before it cracks, same number, different question.
cards.reverse: Two distinct margin conventions, one that shrinks the predicted cycle count by a knockdown, the other that asks how much extra load could be tolerated at a fixed cycle count, that should not be assumed equivalent just because they share a numeric value.

cards.prompt: Fatigue strength correction factor (as a workaround concept)
cards.answer: A manually applied scalar that scales down a room temperature fatigue result to approximate what would happen if the fatigue tool actually accounted for the material being weaker when hot, used whenever the software's built in fatigue model cannot read temperature dependent strain life properties even though the rest of the structural model can.
cards.hook: Picture penciling in a discount on a coupon because the cash register cannot read the hot day barcode, a manual patch for a blind spot in the software.
cards.reverse: A hand applied scaling number used to knock down a room temperature fatigue result when the analysis tool cannot natively read strength properties that vary with temperature.

cards.answer: The stress level at which a metal stops springing back to its original shape once load is removed and starts to deform permanently instead, a property that falls sharply as the metal gets hotter and that governs how much of a hot cycle is spent in plastic deformation.
cards.hook: Picture bending a metal spoon just past the point it stops springing back and stays bent, that threshold stress is yield.
cards.reverse: The stress at which a metal quits returning to its original shape and begins permanent deformation, a property that drops sharply as temperature climbs.

cards.answer: The maximum stress a material can carry in a tension test before it begins to neck down and eventually breaks, used in high temperature fatigue work as a stand in for how much the material's overall strength has degraded at a given temperature.
cards.hook: Picture stretching a rubber band until right before it snaps, that peak load just before failure is the ultimate strength.
cards.reverse: The maximum stress a material withstands in tension before necking and eventually breaking, often used as a proxy for how much strength has been lost at elevated temperature.

cards.answer: The practice of refining a finite element mesh, meaning using smaller and more numerous elements, until further refinement stops changing the computed results by a meaningful amount, which is how an analyst confirms the spatial resolution is fine enough to trust rather than just fast to run.
cards.hook: Picture zooming a photo in tighter and tighter until further zooming stops revealing new detail, that plateau is convergence.
cards.reverse: The check where a finite element grid is refined with smaller elements until the computed results stop changing meaningfully, confirming the resolution can be trusted.

cards.answer: The theoretical gas temperature reached immediately behind a self sustaining detonation wave, calculated from the fuel and oxidizer chemistry, used as the driving hot gas temperature applied as a boundary condition to the wall the detonation sweeps past.
cards.hook: Picture the theoretical scorching ceiling right behind a detonation front, a chemistry calculated number handed to the wall as its hottest boundary condition.
cards.reverse: The predicted gas temperature immediately trailing a self sustaining detonation wave, derived from propellant chemistry and applied as the driving hot side boundary condition on the wall.

cards.answer: A number describing how effectively a moving fluid carries heat into or out of a solid surface, used in a thermal simulation to turn a bulk gas temperature into an actual heat flux hitting the wall, with a bigger number meaning faster heat transfer for the same temperature difference.
cards.hook: Picture a dial that turns how hot the gas is into how fast heat actually punches through the wall, a bigger setting means faster transfer for the same temperature gap.
cards.reverse: A number quantifying how effectively a flowing fluid delivers heat into a solid surface, converting a bulk temperature into an actual flux for a given temperature difference.

cards.answer: The temperature at which a metal alloy first begins to melt, forming a hard ceiling above which any structural or fatigue prediction for the solid part stops being physically meaningful.
cards.hook: Picture the first drop of metal going soft and starting to weep, that onset of melt point is a hard ceiling for any solid state prediction.
cards.reverse: The temperature marking where an alloy first begins to melt, above which structural or fatigue predictions for the solid part no longer apply.

cards.answer: A combined damage mode where slow, time dependent deformation at sustained high temperature, called creep, adds to the cycle by cycle damage from fatigue, so a part exposed to long hot dwell times needs an analysis that adds both effects together rather than treating fatigue alone as the whole story.
cards.hook: Picture a hot metal rod slowly sagging under its own weight between cycles while cracks also grow from the cycling itself, two slow poisons working together.
cards.reverse: A combined damage mechanism where gradual, time dependent deformation under sustained heat compounds with cycle by cycle fatigue damage, relevant whenever a part sits hot for long dwell periods.

cards.answer: Retrieval practice means actively pulling information out of memory by self-quizzing rather than re-reading it. It works because each act of reconstruction is itself a memory-modifying event that strengthens the trace and makes it easier to find later, far more than passive review does.
cards.hook: Picture hauling a bucket up from a deep well by hand instead of just staring down at the water; each haul is what actually brings the fact to the surface.
cards.reverse: Name for boosting memory by forcing yourself to produce an answer from scratch, instead of just reviewing it passively.

cards.answer: Spacing means spreading study of the same material across sessions over time instead of cramming it into one block. It works because letting a memory partially fade before you retrieve it again forces harder, more durable re-encoding, and the varied contexts add more routes back to the memory.
cards.hook: Picture watering a seedling once a week instead of drowning it in one sitting; the gaps between waterings are what grow deep roots.
cards.reverse: Name for scattering study sessions across days or weeks instead of massing them into one cram session.

cards.answer: Interleaving means mixing different problem types or topics within a session rather than practicing one type to completion first. It works because you must decide which concept or strategy applies before solving, so you practice the selection process itself instead of coasting on the previous answer.
cards.hook: Picture shuffling flashcards from three different subjects into one deck instead of stacking each subject on its own pile; the shuffle forces you to guess which rule applies before you play the card.
cards.reverse: Term for switching between multiple topics or problem types in the same sitting, forcing you to pick the right approach each time, instead of finishing one type before moving to the next.

cards.answer: Elaborative interrogation means repeatedly asking why a fact is true and answering it as you study. It works because generating a reason integrates the new fact into your existing knowledge, creating extra associative links that act as retrieval cues later.
cards.hook: Picture a toddler tugging your sleeve and asking why five times in a row until the fact finally clicks.
cards.reverse: Name for the habit of interrogating a new fact with repeated why questions until you can justify it yourself.

cards.answer: Self-explanation means explaining the steps of a solution or passage to yourself in your own words as you go. It works because explaining exposes and repairs gaps in your mental model in real time and ties each new step to what you already understand.
cards.hook: Picture teaching an empty chair the steps of a proof out loud; the chair never talks back, so every gap in your logic has nowhere to hide.
cards.reverse: Term for talking yourself through the logic of a problem step by step, out loud or silently, as you work through it.

cards.answer: Desirable difficulties are conditions that deliberately make study harder in productive ways, such as testing, spacing, and interleaving. They work because the same effort that slows apparent learning forces deeper encoding, so they raise long-term retention even though they lower performance during practice.
cards.hook: Picture a trainer loading extra plates on the bar right when you wanted it lighter; the added strain is what builds the muscle that lasts.
cards.reverse: Umbrella term for practice conditions that slow you down in the moment, such as quizzing or spacing sessions out, yet pay off with stronger retention over time.

cards.prompt: Which study techniques actually rank as high utility?
cards.answer: A large evidence review ranked practice testing and distributed practice as high utility, while popular habits like highlighting, re-reading, and summarizing rated low. It matters because it lets you spend effort on the methods the data supports instead of the ones that merely feel productive.
cards.hook: Picture a highlighter and a stopwatch in a boxing ring; the stopwatch, standing for testing and spacing, wins every round while the highlighter gets knocked out cold.

cards.answer: The generation effect is that producing an answer yourself is remembered better than reading the same answer given to you. It works because generation engages more active encoding and commits you to a candidate, so the correct information sticks to the effort you spent, and even a corrected wrong guess helps.
cards.hook: Picture baking your own loaf of bread from scratch versus buying one already sliced at the store; the loaf you kneaded yourself is the one whose recipe you actually remember.
cards.reverse: Name for the finding that memory favors an answer you generated over the identical answer someone simply showed you.

cards.answer: Deep encoding means processing material for its meaning rather than its surface form when you learn it. It works because durability is a byproduct of depth: semantic analysis builds a richer and more distinctive trace, and depth at encoding predicts recall better than time spent or intent to learn.
cards.hook: Picture two divers at the same lake, one skimming the surface with a snorkel and one diving to the meaning-rich seabed; only the deep diver surfaces holding pearls.
cards.reverse: Term for processing new material by focusing on its underlying meaning rather than its surface appearance, which the levels-of-processing framework says drives durable recall.

cards.answer: Dual coding means pairing verbal information with a matching image or diagram. It works because verbal and visual material use partly separate channels, so encoding both gives you two independent routes to the same memory, and if one fails at recall the other can still succeed.
cards.hook: Picture a word and its picture holding hands like two lifelines thrown to a drowning memory; if one lets go, the other still pulls it ashore.
cards.reverse: Name for pairing a verbal fact with a matching image so the memory has two separate channels leading back to it.

cards.answer: Chunking means grouping many small items into a few larger meaningful units. It works because working memory holds only about seven independent units at once, and chunking packs more content into each slot by recoding raw items into a single meaningful pattern.
cards.hook: Picture rolling loose coins into a paper tube instead of carrying them jingling one by one in your pocket; the roll is one object your hand can hold even though it hides many coins.
cards.reverse: Term for recoding a pile of small details into fewer, denser units so your limited working memory can hold more at once.

cards.prompt: Method of loci (memory palace), and is memory trainable?
cards.answer: The method of loci places vivid images of items along a familiar route, then you mentally walk the route to recall them, exploiting strong spatial and visual memory. Memory is genuinely trainable: a few weeks of this training reshaped ordinary people's brain connectivity toward the pattern seen in elite memory athletes, with gains lasting months.
cards.hook: Picture pinning a strange image to every doorway of your childhood home, then walking the hallway in your mind to collect them in order; ordinary students who trained this way for weeks grew brain wiring like elite memory athletes.
cards.reverse: Ancient recall trick of mentally storing images at stops along a familiar walk, plus the discovery that weeks of practicing it can reshape an ordinary brain toward expert level memory.

cards.answer: Deliberate practice is focused, feedback-driven repetition aimed at pushing a skill past its current ceiling. It shows memory span is a skill, not a fixed trait: an average person reached a 79-digit span through months of practice by inventing mnemonic encodings, so the limit moves with trained strategy rather than raw capacity.
cards.hook: Picture an ordinary grad student drilling digit strings for months until he can hold 79 numbers in his head, proof the memory ceiling was just an untrained plateau.
cards.reverse: Term for targeted, feedback heavy repetition aimed at breaking through a current skill ceiling, shown by an ordinary person stretching digit span into the seventies through months of practice.

cards.answer: Sustained demanding use of a memory skill physically remodels the brain regions that support it. London taxi drivers who memorize the whole city have enlarged posterior hippocampi that scale with years on the job, direct structural evidence that the memory-supporting brain is shaped by practice rather than fixed.
cards.hook: Picture a London cabbie's hippocampus slowly swelling like a trained muscle after years of memorizing every street in the city; the brain scan literally shows the mileage.
cards.reverse: Term describing how years of demanding mental practice physically remodel the brain areas that support that skill, evidenced by taxi drivers' enlarged hippocampi.

cards.answer: You mentally place the things you want to remember at distinct spots along a familiar route or building, then walk the route to retrieve them in order. It works because your spatial memory is strong and stable, so each fragile fact gets a fixed location to live at.
cards.hook: Picture dropping a wild image at every doorway of your childhood house, then walking the hallway later to pick each one back up in order.
cards.reverse: Technique of assigning each item to a specific spot along a route you know well, then walking that route in your mind to retrieve them in sequence.

cards.answer: For a hard term you pick a familiar word that sounds like part of it, then picture that word interacting with the term's meaning. The sound link plus the vivid image chain the new word to things you already know, which is why it works so well for foreign and technical vocabulary.
cards.hook: Picture the Spanish word carta as a playing card mailed inside a letter; the sound alike keyword plus that image is the whole trick.
cards.reverse: Vocabulary technique of linking a foreign or technical word to a familiar sound alike word, then picturing that word acting out the term's meaning.

cards.answer: You pre-memorize rhyming number pegs like one-is-a-bun and two-is-a-shoe, then hang each list item on the peg for its position with an image. The permanent rhyming hooks give numbered or ranked lists a ready-made order to attach to.
cards.hook: Picture a bun skewered on a hook labeled number one; you can hang any grocery item on that permanent bun shaped peg.
cards.reverse: System built on memorized rhyming tags for each number, such as bun for one or shoe for two, that give a ranked list ready made hooks to hang images on.

cards.answer: You map each digit to a consonant sound, then turn number strings into words and images by adding vowels. It works because concrete picturable words are vastly easier to remember than abstract digits, so it is the go-to for long numbers.
cards.hook: Picture the number 32 turning into the word moon because its coded sounds spell it out; suddenly a boring digit string is a night sky.
cards.reverse: System that converts each digit into a consonant sound so long numbers can be turned into ordinary, picturable words.

cards.answer: An acronym packs a list into one word from the first letters, and an acrostic turns those letters into a sentence. Both shrink many items into a single cue and lock in their order, so each initial letter jogs the item it stands for.
cards.hook: Picture the rainbow's colors marching in as a soldier named Roy G Biv; one invented name carries all seven colors in order.
cards.reverse: Memory devices that compress a list into either a single word built from first letters or a sentence built from those letters, locking in the sequence.

cards.answer: You weave separate items into one connected story where each leads to the next, so recalling the narrative pulls out the whole list in sequence. Stories supply causal and temporal links that disconnected lists lack, and connected material is recalled far better.
cards.hook: Picture a grocery list turning into a tiny cartoon where the milk carton trips over the eggs, which roll into the bread; one silly plot drags out the whole list.
cards.reverse: Technique of linking separate items into one continuous narrative so recalling the story pulls out every item in sequence.

cards.prompt: Why do songs, rhythm, and rhyme aid memory?
cards.answer: Setting text to a tune adds redundant cues like melody, meter, and rhyme that segment and constrain the words, and a well-learned tune scaffolds them. Once the melody itself is easy, sung text can be recalled better than the same text merely spoken.
cards.hook: Picture the alphabet song still looping in your head decades after grade school; the tune is a scaffold your memory never had to rebuild.

cards.answer: A mind map is a radiant diagram with a central topic and branches fanning out into sub-topics, using color, keywords, and images. The branching tree shows hierarchy and association spatially, giving each idea a visual place instead of a line of prose.
cards.hook: Picture an octopus with a topic for a head and colored tentacles branching into every sub idea; you can see the whole animal at a glance.
cards.reverse: Diagram technique that puts one core idea in the center and lets colored, keyword labeled branches radiate outward into related subtopics.

cards.prompt: Memory as spatial structure (boxes, trees, files)
cards.answer: You picture knowledge as physical containers such as a filing cabinet or nested boxes and file each fact into a visible slot. Giving abstract knowledge a concrete where turns retrieval into going to the right container rather than searching blank memory.
cards.hook: Picture a filing cabinet drawer labeled thermodynamics where every fact has its own hanging folder; you retrieve by walking to the drawer, not by searching blank air.
cards.reverse: Mental strategy of picturing facts as objects sorted into physical containers, such as drawers or nested boxes, giving abstract knowledge a concrete address.

cards.answer: Encoding an item as a strange, exaggerated, or absurd image can make it stick better than an ordinary one. The real driver is distinctiveness, so the trick works best when the bizarre item stands out among plain ones on a free-recall test.
cards.hook: Picture a giraffe in a business suit riding the subway among a row of ordinary commuters; the one absurd rider is the only face you remember getting off.
cards.reverse: Finding that an unusually weird or exaggerated mental image is often recalled better than a plain one, mostly because it stands out from its neighbors.

cards.answer: Information tied to yourself, your experiences or opinions, is remembered better than the same information judged only for meaning. The self is a rich organizing structure, so relating material to it produces deeper, more connected encoding.
cards.hook: Picture stamping your own name on a fact like a passport stamp; anything tagged mine seems to clear memory customs faster than a stranger's luggage.
cards.reverse: Finding that facts connected to your own identity and experience are remembered better than the same facts evaluated only for what they mean.

cards.answer: You pair a verbal label with a concrete mental picture so an idea is stored in both a word code and an image code. Two independent traces give two retrieval routes, and turning an abstract term into a picture reliably boosts recall.
cards.hook: Picture zipping a word and its picture together like the two rows of a zipper's teeth; pull on either side and the whole memory closes shut around both.
cards.reverse: Practice of pairing a spoken or written label with a concrete mental image so the idea is stored through two separate, mutually reinforcing codes.

cards.prompt: Who is credited with the method of loci, and how?
cards.answer: Tradition credits the Greek poet Simonides of Ceos, who identified guests crushed at a banquet by recalling where each had been seated. The story anchors the classical art of memory, where imagined places store ordered information.
cards.hook: Picture a banquet hall roof collapsing the instant after a poet steps outside, and him rebuilding the guest list purely by recalling who sat where.

cards.answer: Elaboration means processing new material by connecting it to existing knowledge, making it personal, vivid, bizarre, or emotional rather than repeating it flat. The deeper and more connected the processing, the stronger and more retrievable the memory.
cards.hook: Picture wrapping a bare fact in colorful gift paper made from your own memories and opinions before handing it off to long term memory.
